---
date: 2026-02-17
published-title: "Last updated on"
abstract: |
  This part gives a not so short introduction to `Quarto`. This introduction is based on the [quarto.org](https://quarto.org/docs/authoring/markdown-basics.html) website. Most sub-sections provide the hyperlink to the respective `Quarto` sub-website.  
---

# A not so short introduction to Quarto

::: {.callout-tip collapse="show" title="Tip for RMarkdown user"}
If you are familiar with RMarkdown [@R-rmarkdown] the [FAQ for RMarkdown
Users](https://quarto.org/docs/faq/rmarkdown.html) might be interesting
for you.
:::

## What is Quarto? {#what-is-quarto}

![](/software/images/quarto.png){.column-body}

In short, Quarto is a versatile publishing system that allows you to create reports while integrating code and its output. Quarto is based on **Pandoc** which is a universal document converter that transforms files from one markup format into another (e.g., `Markdown` &rarr; `HTML` or `Markdown` &rarr; `PDF`).

::: {.column-margin}
see <https://pandoc.org/> for more information on Pandoc

:::


This means that with Quarto you can create different types of documents, including:

::: {.column-margin}

For an overview about all document types see [quarto.org/docs/guide/](<https://quarto.org/docs/guide/>)

:::

- regular reports,
- presentations (e.g., PowerPoint, Beamer, Reveal.js),
- websites (e.g., regular websites, blogs),
- books, and
- interactive components (e.g., dashboards, Shiny apps),

while *speaking* only one language: **Markdown**^[A brief introduction to the Markdown language can be found in the [Markdown language](#markdown-language) section below.].


Depending on the document type, you can use different output formats (e.g., `HTML`, `PDF`, `MS Word`, `Markdown`).

::: {.column-margin}

An overview about all formats can be found at <https://quarto.org/docs/output-formats/all-formats.html>

:::


## How to work with Quarto?

Working with Quarto is basically the same as working in a regular R-script, except that your file has the extension `.qmd` (instead of `.R`) and needs the following components:

- [YAML header](#yaml-header),
- [body](#quarto-body), and
- [Code chunks](#quarto-computations) for code execution.


::: {.callout-caution title="Exercise: Create your first Quarto document and render it to HTML!" collapse="true"}

First, create a dedicated folder (e.g., `quarto_intro`).

::: {.panel-tabset}

### RStudio

{{< video /software/videos/create_qmd_rstudio.mov >}}

### Positron

{{< video /software/videos/create_qmd_positron.mov >}}

:::



:::


## The YAML header {#yaml-header}

The `YAML` header is enclosed by three dashes (`---`):

::: {.column-margin}

YAML is an acronym for Yet Another Markup Language see <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/YAML>

:::

```{r}
#| label: yaml-engine
#| echo: false
knitr::knit_engines$set(yaml = function(options) {
  knitr::engine_output(options, options$code, "")
})
# hacky to avoid warnings when rendering the document, yaml code is not executed, but only shown as code block
```

```{yaml}
#| label: yaml-header
#| eval: false
#| echo: true
---
title: "i like quarto"
format: html
---
```

### Overview of YAML syntax

The basic syntax of `YAML` uses so-called key-value pairs in the format `key: value`. This is powerful, because you can generate great documents like this `Quarto book` with only a few lines of code in the `YAML` header. Some useful key-value pairs are the following:

```{yaml}
#| label: yaml-key-value
#| eval: false
#| echo: true
subtitle: "my subtitle"
author:
  - "John Doe"
  - "Jane Roe"
  - "Norah Jones"
date: today
toc: true
toc-title: "Inhaltsverzeichnis"
number-sections: true
```

::: {.callout-caution collapse="true"}
## Exercise: Identify useful key-value pairs (5min)

1.  Check out [quarto.org](https://quarto.org) and identify useful `key: value` pairs. It is important to note that there are sometimes different `YAML` options for the formats:
    -   [HTML](https://quarto.org/docs/output-formats/html-basics.html)
    -   [PDF](https://quarto.org/docs/output-formats/pdf-basics.html)

2.  Add them to your YAML header.

:::

### Bibliography and Citation Style

With Quarto it is possible to automatically generate citations and bibliographies in many different ways. The `key: value` pair to include a reference list in Quarto is:

::: {.column-margin}

<https://quarto.org/docs/authoring/footnotes-and-citations.html>

:::



```{yaml}
#| label: yaml-citation
#| eval: false
#| echo: true
#| code-line-numbers: false
bibliography: r-refs.bib
```

Here we use a so-called BibLaTex (`.bib`) file. These files can be generated with the most reference management software programs
(e.g., [Citavi](https://www.citavi.com/en), [EndNote](https://endnote.com/), [zotero](https://www.zotero.org/), ...).


A reference in the .bib-file looks like this:

``` bib
@Manual{R-base,
  title = {R: A Language and Environment for Statistical Computing},
  author = {{R Core Team}},
  organization = {R Foundation for Statistical Computing},
  address = {Vienna, Austria},
  year = {2022},
  url = {https://www.R-project.org/},
}
```

::: {.callout-caution collapse="true"}
## Exercise: Create a .bib file for your R references


1.  Save (some of the) R packages in a character vector.

```{r}
#| label: demo-pkgs
#| echo: true
#| eval: false
#| code-fold: false
pkgList <- c("knitr", # tables
             "kableExtra", # more tables
             "psych", # descriptive
             "lavaan") # SEM
```

2.  Use the `write_bib()` function from the `knitr` package [@R-knitr].

```{r}
#| label: demo-write-bib
#| eval: false
#| echo: true
#| code-fold: false

# getwd() # check where the .bib file is saved
knitr::write_bib(x = pkgList,
                 file = "r-refs.bib")
```

It is important to note that `.bib` file needs to be in the same folder as the `.qmd` file, otherwise you need to provide the path to the `.bib` file.

:::

To change the citation style (e.g., APA, Chicago Manual of Style), we use the `csl key`:

::: {.column-margin}

CSL is the abbreviation for Citation Style Language (for more see <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Citation_Style_Language>)

<br><br>

The `apa.csl` file can be found on github: <https://github.com/citation-style-language/styles>.

:::

```{yaml}
#| label: yaml-csl
#| eval: false
#| echo: true
#| code-line-numbers: false
csl: apa.csl
```



The citation syntax (i.e., how to cite the references in the text) is explained in the subsection [Citation syntax](#cite-syn).

### Execution/Output options

::: {.column-margin}

For an overview of (chunk) options see [quarto.org/docs/computations/execution-options](https://quarto.org/docs/computations/execution-options.html).

:::

To customize code execution or output behavior, the options can be specified either:

-   globally (see in the following) or
-   per code block (see the section on [Chunk options](#comp-chunk-opt))

In the following example, we set `echo: false` and `warning: false`.

```{yaml}
#| label: yaml-exec-opt
#| eval: false
#| echo: true
#| code-line-numbers: false
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
```



This means that no code and no warnings are shown in the entire document unless you explicitly override these settings in a specific code block.



## The body {#quarto-body}

When the YAML header is set up, we can work on the body of the document. In the body, we write the text and include the code chunks. The body can be structured with headings, lists, images, tables, and so on. To do this, we use the **Markdown language**^[It is also possible to use `Latex` or `HTML` syntax. However, you most likely run into problems when rendering into the respective other type.] which is lightweight markup language with plain-text formatting syntax. 

::: {.column-margin}

<https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Markdown>

:::




### Markdown language {#markdown-language}

There are plenty of different guides to get a quick overview about the language (see e.g.,
[markdownguide](https://www.markdownguide.org/basic-syntax/) or [Quarto-website](https://quarto.org/docs/authoring/markdown-basics.html)).
The following code snippets are copied and sometimes slightly adjusted from [quarto.org](https://quarto.org/docs/authoring/markdown-basics.html).

::: {.callout-caution collapse="true"}
## Exercise: Make yourself familiar with the Markdown language (15min)

1.  [Text formatting](#md-txt-format)

2.  [Headings](#md-head)

3.  [Lists](#md-lists)

:::

#### Text formatting {#md-txt-format}

+----------------------------------+------------------------------+
| Markdown Syntax                  | Output                       |
+==================================+==============================+
| ```                              | *italics* and **bold**       |
| *italics* and **bold**           |                              |
| ```                              |                              |
+----------------------------------+------------------------------+
| ```                              | superscript^2^ /             |
| superscript^2^ / subscript~2~    | subscript~2~                 |
| ```                              |                              |
+----------------------------------+------------------------------+
| ```                              | ~~strikethrough~~            |
| ~~strikethrough~~                |                              |
| ```                              |                              |
+----------------------------------+------------------------------+
| ```                              | `verbatim code`              |
| `verbatim code`                  |                              |
| ```                              |                              |
+----------------------------------+------------------------------+

#### Headings {#md-head}

To create headings, add one (or more) `#` in front of the heading.

+--------------------+----------------------------------------------+
| Markdown Syntax    | Output                                       |
+====================+==============================================+
| ```                | # Header 1 {.heading-output .unnumbered}     |
| # Header 1         |                                              |
| ```                |                                              |
+--------------------+----------------------------------------------+
| ```                | ## Header 2 {.heading-output .unnumbered}    |
| ## Header 2        |                                              |
| ```                |                                              |
+--------------------+----------------------------------------------+
| ```                | ### Header 3 {.heading-output .unnumbered}   |
| ### Header 3       |                                              |
| ```                |                                              |
+--------------------+----------------------------------------------+

```{=html}
<style type="text/css">
.heading-output {
  border-bottom: none;
  margin-top: 0;
  margin-bottom: 0;
}
</style>
```

This goes up to level 6 (i.e., `######`) which probably is not recommended to use though.

#### Lists {#md-lists}

+------------------------------------+--------------------------------+
| Markdown Syntax                    | Output                         |
+====================================+================================+
| ```                                | -   unordered list             |
| * unordered list                   |                                |
|     + sub-item 1                   |     -   sub-item 1             |
|     + sub-item 2                   |                                |
|         - sub-sub-item 1           |     -   sub-item 2             |
| ```                                |                                |
|                                    |         -   sub-sub-item 1     |
+------------------------------------+--------------------------------+
| ```                                | 1.  ordered list               |
| 1. ordered list                    |                                |
| 2. item 2                          | 2.  item 2                     |
|     i) sub-item 1                  |                                |
|          A.  sub-sub-item 1        |     i)  sub-item 1             |
| ```                                |                                |
|                                    |         A.  sub-sub-item 1     |
+------------------------------------+--------------------------------+

::: {.column-margin}

<br><br>

More example on how to create lists can be found here <https://quarto.org/docs/authoring/markdown-basics.html#lists>

:::

### Cross-referencing

Cross-referencing is a useful feature to reference headings, figures, tables, equations (called objects in the following), etc within a Quarto project. To make an object referenceable, we need to provide unique identifiers for the respective object. Identifiers **must** start with their type (e.g., `#fig-`, `#tbl-`, `#eq-`), except for headings.

::: {.column-margin}

For more on cross-referencing see <https://quarto.org/docs/authoring/cross-references.html>.

:::


This is done by adding a label in curly braces `{}` after the object or section. The label needs to start with the type of the object (e.g., `#fig-` for figures, `#tbl-` for tables, `#eq-` for equations), except for headings where the label can be freely chosen.


When we want to cross-reference the **Images** section, we have to
provide unique identifier that is enclosed by braces `{}` (here:
`#include-images`) after the heading:

```{r}
#| label: md-engine
#| echo: false
knitr::knit_engines$set(markdown = function(options) {
  knitr::engine_output(options, options$code, "")
})
# hacky to avoid warnings when rendering the document, md code is not executed, but only shown as code block
```

```{markdown}
#| label: md-headings
#| eval: false
#| code-line-numbers: false

## Images {#include-images} 
```

An example on how to cross-reference figures is shown in the [Images](#include-images) section.



### Images {#include-images}

The Markdown way to include images is as follows:

```{markdown}
#| code-line-numbers: false
![A pinguin who likes pressing buttons](/path-to-file/gif-coding-penguins.gif){#fig-pinguin}
```

The caption `[A pinguin who likes pressing buttons]` and label `{#fig-pinguin}` makes the figure referenceable. To reference it, use the following syntax:

::: {.column-margin}

See <https://quarto.org/docs/authoring/figures.html> for more.

:::

```{markdown}
#| code-line-numbers: false
See @fig-pinguin for a pinguin who likes pressing buttons.
```

This renders to:

See @fig-pinguin for a pinguin who likes pressing buttons.

![A pinguin who likes pressing buttons](/software/images/gif-coding-penguins.gif){#fig-pinguin width="80%"}

::: {.callout-tip title="There are more options to include images..." collapse="true"}

For example, the `include_graphics` function from the `knitr` package [@R-knitr]:

```{r}
#| label: fig-demo-include
#| eval: false
#| fig-cap: "The caption goes here"
#| fig-cap-location: top
#| echo: fenced

knitr::include_graphics("path-to-file")
```

It is made referenceable via the `label: fig-demo-include`. 


:::

### Citation syntax {#cite-syn}

Recall a reference in the .bib-file looks like this:

``` bib
@Manual{R-base,
  title = {R: A Language and Environment for Statistical Computing},
  author = {{R Core Team}},
  organization = {R Foundation for Statistical Computing},
  address = {Vienna, Austria},
  year = {2022},
  url = {https://www.R-project.org/},
}
```

::: {.column-margin}

For more on the citation syntax see the [Pandoc User Guide](https://pandoc.org/MANUAL.html#citations).

:::


For example, to cite R, we need to use `@` before the so-called BibLaTex key `R-base`. Three example follow:

1.  `[@R-base]` renders as: [@R-base]
2.  `@R-base` renders as: @R-base
3.  `[-@R-base]` renders as: [-@R-base]

Multiple citations are separated by semicolons:

4.  `[@R-base; @R-knitr]` renders as: [@R-base; @R-knitr]


### There is a lot more!

-   Technical writing (e.g., Equations): Use `$` delimiters for inline
    math and `$$` delimiters for display math (see
    [https://quarto.org](https://quarto.org/docs/visual-editor/technical.html){target="_blank"})

-   Links:

    -   `[This is a link to google.de](www.google.de)` which appears as
        [This is a link to google.de](www.google.de)
    -   `<https://quarto.org>` which appears as <https://quarto.org>

-   Diagrams (e.g.,
    [Mermaid](https://mermaid.js.org/#/){target="_blank"},[Graphviz](https://graphviz.org/){target="_blank"})

-   Videos

-   ...

## Computations {#quarto-computations}

::: {.callout-note title="engine: knitr" collapse="true"}

To execute R code, Quarto uses the [knitr](https://yihui.org/knitr/) engine . However, Quarto also supports the Jupyter engine (for executing [Python](https://www.python.org/) code) and the Julia engine (for executing [Julia](https://julialang.org/) code).

```{yaml}
#| label: yaml-engines
#| eval: false
#| echo: true
---
engine: knitr # default for R code execution
---
```

:::



### R code blocks (or chunks) {#comp-codeblock}

Within a `Quarto` document source code can be displayed by using ```` ``` ```` at the start and the end of the code. The starting ```` ``` ```` are followed by braces around the programming
language (e.g., ```` ```{python} ````). `R` code is included by using curly braces around the **letter r** (i.e., ```` ```{r} ````)


This looks like this:

```{r}
#| label: fig-example-ggplot
#| echo: fenced
#| fig-cap: "My first ggplot"
#| results: hold


library(ggplot2)

ggplot(data = diamonds,
       aes(y = carat, x = price, color = cut)) +
  geom_point() +
  labs(y = "Carat", x = "Price", color = "Cut") +
  theme_classic()


```

::: {.callout-caution title="Exercise: Create your first R code block."}

Keyboard shortcuts: 

- Windows: {{< kbd Shift-Control-I >}}

- Mac: {{< kbd mac=Shift-Command-I >}}

:::

### Chunk options {#comp-chunk-opt}

What are chunk options? Chunk options customize the output of the code blocks. In `Quarto` it is recommended^[The `rmarkdown` syntax is different (e.g., `{r my-label, echo = FALSE}`), but it also works in `Quarto`.] to include the chunk options
as special comments (i.e., `|#`) at the top of the chunk.

In the following example, we set `output: false`...

```{r}
#| label: my-first-chunk
#| echo: fenced
#| output: false

print("hello world!")
```

... and hence, no output is shown.

The most common chunk options are:

+---------------+----------------------------------+------------------+
| Chunk option  | Description                      | Value            |
+===============+==================================+==================+
| `echo`        | Include the source code in       | `tr              |
|               | output                           | ue/false/fenced` |
+---------------+----------------------------------+------------------+
| `eval`        | Evaluate the code chunk. If      | `true/false`     |
|               | `false` the code of the chunk    |                  |
|               | will not be executed, but        |                  |
|               | depending on the `echo` value be |                  |
|               | displayed or not.                |                  |
+---------------+----------------------------------+------------------+
| `include`     | Include source code &(!) results | `true/false`     |
|               | in the output.                   |                  |
+---------------+----------------------------------+------------------+
| `results`     | Should results be displayed in   | `ma              |
|               | the output or not (`false`)? If  | rkup/asis/hold/` |
|               | yes how (`markup` vs. `asis`)?   | `hide/false`     |
+---------------+----------------------------------+------------------+
| `warnings`    | Include warnings in output.      | `true/false`     |
+---------------+----------------------------------+------------------+

## Rendering

There are multiple ways to render a `Quarto` document:

::: {.column-margin}

<https://quarto.org/docs/computations/r.html#rendering>

:::

1.  Clicking the **Render** (RStudio) / **Preview** (Positron) Button or using keyboard shortcuts (windows: {{< kbd Ctrl-Shift-K >}}, mac:  {{< kbd mac=Shift-Command-K >}})

2.  Using the terminal

    -   `quarto render script.qmd --to html`
    -   `quarto render script.qmd --to pdf`
    -   ...

3.  Using the `quarto_render()` function from the `quarto` [@R-quarto]
    package



::: {.callout-important}

When rendering a `Quarto` document, all code chunks are executed (except you stated: `eval: false`). This means whenever your code fails, the rendering process will fail, too. Moreover, long-running code (e.g., simulations) should be done in a separate session, and the results should be saved (e.g., as `.rds` file). Then, in the `Quarto` document, you can import the results (e.g., with `readRDS()`) and process them (e.g., create tables or figures).

:::
